summary: This guest post by Jordan Ellenberg explores why the Stacks Project's dependency graph shows a nearly linear relationship between vertices and edges. He hypothesizes this might be an expected property of random directed acyclic graphs rather than revealing deep mathematical structure. Using Sage, he generates random graphs with decaying connection probabilities and finds similar linear patterns. However, he notes his random models produce graphs with substantial overlap between descendant subgraphs, which may not reflect the actual Stacks Project structure.
key_points: The Stacks Project graph of 10,445 mathematical assertions shows a surprisingly linear relationship between vertex and edge counts in subgraphs
Random directed acyclic graphs generated with decaying connection probabilities also exhibit this near-linear property
The linear pattern persists whether using exponential decay or power-law decay for connection probabilities
Random models show high overlap between descendant graphs, but the Stacks Project may not share this property
A 2D lattice model with less overlap might better test whether linearity is truly generic
money_quotes: The Stacks Project autovomits out any contribution that would create a logical cycle! I wish LaTeX could do that.
Why is this so? Does it suggest some underlying structure? I tend to say no, or at least not much — my guess is that in some sense it is 'expected' for graphs like this to have this sort of property.
My guess: a random graph aficionado could prove that the plot stays very close to a line with high probability under a broad range of random graph models.
A vertex is very likely to be connected all but c of the vertices below it for a suitable constant c.
In her scatterplot we are not measuring the same graph again and again.
key_insight: The linear relationship between edges and vertices in mathematical dependency graphs may be a generic property of random directed acyclic graphs rather than evidence of deep structural organization.
